rows.change_reason: S151: Strengthen weak assertion (common word -> specific identifier)
rows.description: The specification is the mutual understanding between owner and Claude of what is being built. When the owner requests a change or identifies a flaw, Claude first priority is to create or update a specification that captures the owner intent. Testing and implementation proceed only after mutual understanding is established through the specification. Specifications are the negotiation artifact — not a compliance layer applied after implementation.
rows.title: Specs are the negotiation artifact for mutual understanding

rows.change_reason: S151: Strengthen weak assertion (common word -> specific identifier)
rows.description: Specifications record the agreement between owner and Claude about what is being implemented. They are as detailed and specific as business goals require — some broad, some granular. The binding constraint is that every test derived from a specification MUST produce an unambiguous PASS/FAIL result. When ambiguity is discovered during testing or owner review, the specification is refined until the ambiguity is resolved. Specifications evolve through use — not through upfront decomposition.
rows.title: Spec granularity driven by test unambiguity, not mandatory decomposition

rows.change_reason: S151: Strengthen weak assertion (common word -> specific identifier)
rows.description: Specifications tolerate ambiguity and improve over time. The initial specification represents the clearest mutual understanding achievable at the time of writing. When owner testing reveals a gap between intent and implementation — even when Claude automated tests pass — the specification is refined to eliminate the discovered ambiguity. This iterative refinement is the normal maturation process of an implementation, not a defect in the specification process.
rows.title: Specs mature through iterative refinement

rows.change_reason: S151: Strengthen weak assertion (common word -> specific identifier)
rows.description: When testing reveals an error or omission, the first priority is correcting the specification — not the implementation. Adding specificity and removing ambiguity from the spec produces new or revised tests that automatically identify incorrect implementation. The implementation is then updated to pass the revised tests. This spec-first correction cycle may iterate until ambiguity is resolved. Jumping straight to a code fix without updating the spec leaves the same ambiguity in place for future drift.
rows.title: Spec-first correction cycle: fix the spec, not the code

rows.change_reason: Auto-generated assertion from keyword mapping
rows.description: When a bug or defect is discovered during a testing or spec-maturation session: (1) Review the relevant specification. (2) Review the relevant test. (3) If the bug is legitimate, record it as a work item in the Knowledge Database backlog (status: specified). (4) Do NOT fix the bug in the current session. Bug fixes occur in separate implementation sessions, each following the spec-first workflow (update spec, derive test, implement, verify). Rationale: Fixing bugs during testing bypasses the specification discipline. Without the spec update, the fix is uncontrolled and may regress in a future session with no detection mechanism. The backlog ensures every defect is recorded with enough context to fix correctly later.
rows.title: No bug fixes during testing procedures

rows.assertions: [{"type": "grep", "file": "src/chat/pipeline/agent_dispatch.py", "pattern": "_call_via_transport", "description": "Transport dispatch function exists in agent_dispatch"}, {"type": "grep", "file": "src/chat/pipeline/agent_dispatch.py", "pattern": "generate_stream", "description": "RG streaming path exists (per-interface policy)"}, {"type": "grep", "file": "src/multi_tenant/agntcy_sdk_integration.py", "pattern": "create_a2a_client", "description": "A2A client factory exists for transport dispatch"}]
rows.change_reason: Option B Phase 1: add executable assertions for per-interface transport dispatch
rows.description:
Architecture Decision Record: Per-interface transport dispatch.

## Decision (amended 2026-03-28)

The dispatch algorithm for inter-agent communication uses per-interface transport selection:

### Non-streaming agents (IC, KR, CR, EH, AN)
1. If SLIM/gRPC relay is physically reachable -> use SLIM.
2. Else if NATS broker is physically reachable -> use NATS.
3. Else if HTTP(S) endpoint is physically reachable -> use HTTP(S).
4. Else -> fail with 503 and tier-diagnostic.

### RG token streaming
Gateway in-process ResponseGeneratorAgent.generate_stream() is the intended production path.
This is a per-interface decision, not a fallback (DCL-002 v4, owner-approved 2026-03-28).

## Context

Original ADR-001 v2 described a single global cascade. Container Apps outbound streaming instability
(INSIGHTS-2026-03-28-01-10) required a per-interface exception for RG streaming.

## Consequences

- Non-streaming dispatch remains fully containerized and transport-agnostic.
- RG streaming is co-located with the gateway process for reliability.
- _call_response_generator_stream_direct() is the canonical RG streaming path, not dead code.
- _call_intent_classifier_direct() and _call_knowledge_retrieval_direct() remain dead code pending Phase 3 cleanup.
rows.title: Per-interface transport dispatch (amended for RG streaming)

rows.assertions: [{"type": "grep", "file": "src/agents/containers/agent_app.py", "pattern": "create_agent_app", "description": "Agent container app factory exists"}, {"type": "grep", "file": "scripts/agent-container-template.yaml", "pattern": "targetPort", "description": "Container template defines port exposure"}, {"type": "grep", "file": "scripts/deploy_agent_containers.py", "pattern": "AGENTS", "description": "Agent container deployment script defines agent mapping"}]
rows.change_reason: Fix assertion: targetPort not containerPort in agent-container-template.yaml
rows.description:
Architecture Decision Record: Each pipeline agent operates in a dedicated container. No shared-process agent hosting in the canonical architecture.

## Decision

The mandatory container set for the first recovery tranche:

| Agent | Container Name | Role |
|-------|---------------|------|
| Intent Classifier (IC) | agent-red-ic | Classifies inbound message intent |
| Knowledge Retrieval (KR) | agent-red-kr | Retrieves knowledge articles for RAG |
| Response Generator (RG) | agent-red-rg | Generates customer-facing responses |
| Critic Supervisor | agent-red-critic | Reviews response quality before delivery |
| Escalation Handler | agent-red-escalation | Routes to human agents |
| Analytics Collector | agent-red-analytics | Aggregates conversation metrics |

Deferred: Co-pilot (not in first recovery tranche per owner decision).

Widget: Edge client (not a container). Its traffic path must traverse the canonical containerized agent system. Widget-origin requests are traced through the same dispatch path as API-origin requests.

## Context

Prior state: All agents co-located in gateway process with in-process dispatch. Owner directive (2026-03-27): each agent must operate in a dedicated container. Recovery scoped to 6 pipeline-critical agents plus widget path.

## Failed Approaches

1. Monolithic gateway with in-process agents: No fault isolation, no independent scaling, phantom verification of transport.

## Rejected Alternatives

1. Shared multi-agent containers (2-3 agents per container): Partial isolation but still couples failure domains.
2. Serverless functions per agent: Latency overhead too high for real-time conversation pipeline.

## Consequences

- Each container needs its own Dockerfile, health endpoint, and agent card.
- Inter-agent communication is always over transport (SLIM/NATS/HTTP), never in-process.
- Container failure is isolated — one agent down does not take down the pipeline.
- Resource allocation (CPU/memory) is per-agent, enabling independent scaling.
- Build pipeline produces 7 images (gateway + 6 agents) instead of 1.

## Linked Specs

SPEC-1802, SPEC-1535, SPEC-1536, SPEC-1537
rows.title: Dedicated per-agent containers

rows.assertions: [{"description": "IntentRouter exists as execution-plan boundary after IC", "type": "behavioral"}, {"description": "Core pipeline is one route target, not the only route representation", "type": "behavioral"}, {"description": "No generic DAG engine is implemented in Phases 0-2", "type": "behavioral"}]
rows.change_reason: ADR for IntentRouter vs full DAG decision per Codex advisory review
rows.description:
Decision: Use IntentRouter as the execution-plan boundary for routing customer-originated traffic to core pipeline, co-pilot, or peer agents. Do not implement a full DAG engine.

Context: The existing orchestrator has a fixed 6-agent pipeline with a co-pilot branch. Adding peer agents (campaigns, sales, bot, etc.) requires a routing mechanism. Two options were evaluated: (A) IntentRouter as execution planner, (B) full pipeline DAG.

Decision rationale:
- Option A (IntentRouter) has lowest rework against current orchestrator.
- The co-pilot branch is a proven routing pattern in this codebase.
- A full DAG front-loads cost into the least validated part of the proposal.
- IntentRouter route descriptors are consumable by a later DAG if needed (high reversibility).

Rejected alternative: Full pipeline DAG now (Option B). Too much architectural churn relative to verified immediate need. Current dashboards, trace schema, session semantics, and routing code are not ready for that jump.

Failed approaches: None yet (this is a forward-looking decision).

Consequences:
- Phase 0/1 must establish identity, binding, and trace contracts before IntentRouter (Phase 2).
- The orchestrator retains its fixed core sequence as one route target.
- Peer agents are independently routable but do not form a generic graph.
- If dynamic multi-agent chaining is needed later, IntentRouter can be replaced with a DAG engine that consumes the same route descriptors.

References: Codex advisory review (INSIGHTS-2026-03-28-22-27), Option A vs B analysis.
rows.title: IntentRouter as execution-plan boundary for agent extensibility

rows.assertions: [{"text": "The primary key for customer identity MUST be an internally generated unique ID, not a contact means", "type": "requirement"}, {"text": "Email address MUST be an attribute of identity, not the identity itself", "type": "requirement"}, {"text": "Phone number MUST be an attribute of identity, not the identity itself", "type": "requirement"}, {"text": "A contact attribute MAY be changed or replaced without altering the customer unique identity", "type": "requirement"}, {"text": "Lookup by any verified contact attribute MUST resolve to the same canonical identity", "type": "requirement"}, {"text": "Customer tokens MUST carry the canonical ID as the primary claim", "type": "requirement"}]
rows.change_reason: Full implementation complete (9/9 WIs, 4 phases, 46 tests, Codex 2 review rounds). v1.98.87 deployed. DEKs provisioned. ZK Pillar 1 active.
rows.description:
## Decision

The primary key for a customer identity MUST be an internally generated, stable, unique identifier — never a contact means.

Contact methods (email, phone number, Shopify customer ID, WhatsApp number) are attributes of an identity, not the identity itself. A contact attribute:
- Can be used to look up or resolve an identity
- Can be changed or replaced without altering the user unique identity in the system
- May be verified (OTP, HMAC) to establish trust, but verification status is a property of the attribute, not of the identity

## Context

The current system uses email address or Shopify customer ID as de facto primary keys for customer identity (session.py:1143-1147 resolves profile as customer_id or email or None). This creates profile fragmentation across channels, inability to add new contact types cleanly, and unstable cache keys.

## Priority

PRE-LAUNCH BLOCKER. This refactor must be completed before marketing spend acquires production users. Migrating identity keys after users are in the system creates data migration risk and profile fragmentation that cannot be cleanly resolved. Same priority class as zero-knowledge security work.

## Consequences

1. Every customer gets a system-generated canonical ID (e.g., cid_<uuid>)
2. Contact methods (email, phone, Shopify ID, WhatsApp) are linked attributes
3. An attribute can be added, changed, or removed without changing the canonical ID
4. Lookup by any verified attribute resolves to the same canonical ID
5. Cross-channel conversations merge under one identity when attributes are linked
6. Customer tokens carry the canonical ID as the primary claim

## Rejected Alternatives

- Email as primary key: breaks when phone-only customers exist or email changes
- Shopify customer ID as primary key: only works for Shopify-authenticated users
- Phone number as primary key: same problem as email — contact method, not identity
- Composite key (email + phone + shopify_id): unstable, hard to normalize

## Status

Decided. Pre-launch implementation required. Identity-contract refactor must precede user acquisition.
rows.title: Canonical Customer Identity: Stable Internal ID with Contact Attributes

rows.assertions: [{"type": "grep", "file": "src/multi_tenant/envelope_encryption.py", "pattern": "encrypt_field", "description": "Envelope encryption field-level encrypt exists"}, {"type": "grep", "file": "src/multi_tenant/audit_sanitizer.py", "pattern": "sanitize", "description": "Audit sanitizer strips PII from logs"}]
rows.change_reason: Option B Phase 2: canonical ADR for zero-knowledge architecture (derives from SPEC-1843, SPEC-1844)
rows.description: The platform operator (superadmin) MUST NOT have the ability to read tenant conversation content, customer PII, knowledge articles, or raw API keys. SPA endpoints return cleansed operational metadata only (conversation counts, error rates, billing status). Envelope encryption with tenant-scoped DEKs ensures data-at-rest is operator-blind. Audit sanitization strips PII from logs.
rows.title: Zero-knowledge operator boundary (operator cannot read tenant data)

rows.assertions: [{"type": "grep", "file": "src/multi_tenant/middleware.py", "pattern": "ar_spa_", "description": "Middleware handles SPA key prefix"}, {"type": "grep", "file": "src/multi_tenant/middleware.py", "pattern": "ar_user_", "description": "Middleware handles tenant user key prefix"}]
rows.change_reason: Option B Phase 2: canonical ADR for SPA auth isolation (derives from SPEC-1667)
rows.description: The SPA provider console authenticates exclusively via platform API keys (ar_spa_*). SPA auth MUST be fully isolated from tenant auth (ar_user_*, ar_live_*). No SPA key can access tenant-scoped endpoints. No tenant key can access SPA-scoped endpoints. The middleware enforces this via separate auth paths with non-overlapping key prefixes.
rows.title: SPA console authentication isolation from tenant auth

Constraint: All specs with ADR-* prefix must be auto-typed as architecture_decision, and all specs with DCL-* prefix must be auto-typed as design_constraint, at insert time.
Scope: tools/knowledge-db/db.py
Derived from: ADR-001
Rationale: Without auto-detection, ADR/DCL specs could be inserted as plain requirements, making them invisible to governance queries and DCL compliance checks.
rows.title: ADR/DCL ID prefix auto-detection must exist in KB API

rows.assertions: [{"type": "grep", "file": "src/chat/pipeline/agent_dispatch.py", "pattern": "_call_via_transport", "description": "IC/KR canonical dispatch routes through transport, not in-process"}, {"type": "grep", "file": "src/chat/pipeline/agent_dispatch.py", "pattern": "generate_stream", "description": "RG streaming uses generate_stream (in-process intended path per DCL-002 v4)"}, {"type": "grep", "file": "src/chat/pipeline/agent_dispatch.py", "pattern": "Per-interface transport policy", "description": "DCL-002 v4 per-interface policy is documented in the module"}]
rows.change_reason: Fix double-encoded assertions JSON
rows.description:
In-process agent dispatch MUST NOT exist in the canonical pipeline for non-streaming interfaces (IC, KR, CR, EH, AN). These agents run in dedicated containers via SLIM/NATS/HTTP cascade per SPEC-1802. RG token streaming uses the gateway in-process ResponseGeneratorAgent.generate_stream() as the intended production path - this is a per-interface transport decision (INSIGHTS-2026-03-28-01-10), not a fallback.

Note: _call_*_direct() stub methods exist in agent_dispatch.py as dead code pending Phase 3 cleanup. Their presence does not violate DCL-002 because they are not called in the canonical dispatch path. The canonical IC/KR path routes through _call_via_transport().
rows.title: No mainline in-process dispatch in the canonical pipeline (non-streaming)

rows.change_reason: S252: Stays specified. Contract twins for external SDK mocks not yet built. Phase 3 WI needed.
rows.description:
Design Constraint: Every mocked external SDK call in the test suite must have a corresponding contract test that validates the mock against the real installed SDK.

## Rationale

Codex Finding 3 (INSIGHTS-2026-03-27-01-00) identified that AGNTCY SDK tests validate mocks and structural source strings, not the installed SDK contract. Mocks can drift from reality silently. Contract twins ensure mocks stay honest.

## Enforcement

Advisory in Phase 1. Becomes blocking in Phase 3 when contract tests are written.

## Linked Specs

SPEC-1524, SPEC-1525, ADR-001
rows.title: Every mocked external SDK call must have a contract twin

rows.assertions: [{"id": "DCL-ADVISORY-ROUTING-001.A1", "kind": "behavioral", "description": "Routing predicate: when latest_status == 'ADVISORY' on a bridge INDEX entry, the cross-harness event-driven trigger SHOULD exclude that entry from the recipient's actionable-signature computation by default; the entry's surfacing path is the in-session AXIS 2 UserPromptSubmit hook (.claude/hooks/bridge-axis-2-surface.py).", "verifying_test": "platform_tests/groundtruth_kb/specs/test_dcl_advisory_routing.py::test_assertions_reference_advisory_precondition"}, {"id": "DCL-ADVISORY-ROUTING-001.A2", "kind": "structural", "description": "Per-parser flexibility hedge: the by-default qualifier preserves freedom for parallel runtime thread gtkb-bridge-advisory-status-001 to specify per-parser dispositions (exclude / include / partially-evaluate) without violating the DCL. Per-parser empirical evidence is the precondition for any future SHOULD-to-MUST promotion.", "verifying_test": "platform_tests/groundtruth_kb/specs/test_dcl_advisory_routing.py::test_should_not_must_wording"}, {"id": "DCL-ADVISORY-ROUTING-001.A3", "kind": "structural", "description": "Constraints JSON contract: row.constraints contains JSON object {'enforcement_mode': 'advisory'}. Enforcement mode is stored under the existing constraints column per live specifications schema; a first-class enforcement_mode column would require a separate schema-extension proposal.", "verifying_test": "platform_tests/groundtruth_kb/specs/test_dcl_advisory_routing.py::test_constraints_enforcement_mode_advisory"}]
rows.change_reason: Insert DCL-ADVISORY-ROUTING-001 per Codex GO at bridge/gtkb-advisory-routing-dcl-004.md (REVISED-1 review of -003); parent Slice-0 GO at bridge/gtkb-advisory-report-message-type-conversion-004.md authorized this follow-on (c).
rows.description:
## Constraint Statement

ADVISORY-status bridge entries (latest INDEX line beginning with `ADVISORY:`) SHOULD be routed via Axis-2 (non-dispatchable) per `.claude/rules/bridge-essential.md` Two-Axis Bridge Automation Model. The cross-harness event-driven trigger (`scripts/cross_harness_bridge_trigger.py`) SHOULD exclude ADVISORY rows from the actionable-signature computation for the receiving harness. ADVISORY surfacing into the interactive chat session is via the in-session AXIS 2 UserPromptSubmit hook (`.claude/hooks/bridge-axis-2-surface.py`) instead.

Exact per-parser runtime disposition (which parsers exclude / include / partially-evaluate ADVISORY rows) is owned by the parallel runtime thread `gtkb-bridge-advisory-status-001`. This DCL describes the design contract intent; it is not a hard runtime mandate at this stage.

## Rationale

- Preserves Axis-2 ownership of non-dispatchable work. ADVISORY entries are not self-contained for a freshly-spawned counterpart harness session; they require interactive owner input or accumulating cross-thread context.
- Avoids double-notify (both fresh-harness dispatch via Axis-1 AND in-session AXIS 2 surface). Either one alone is sufficient signal.
- Avoids advisory thrashing in the actionable-signature computation that Axis-1 dispatch state depends on; ADVISORY rows would otherwise churn the signature on every protocol-extension surface.
- Keeps the SHOULD-to-MUST escalation surface available for empirical-evidence-driven promotion later, gated on the parallel runtime thread.

## Predicate

```
assert (latest_status == "ADVISORY") -> (
    recipient_actionable_signature_excludes_entry_by_default
    AND axis_2_surface_notifies_in_session
)
```

The `_by_default` clause preserves runtime flexibility for the parallel parser thread to specify per-parser dispositions without violating this constraint.

## Enforcement Mode

Phase 1 advisory pilot per `GOV-20`. The mode is stored as `constraints = {"enforcement_mode": "advisory"}` JSON because the live `specifications` table schema does not include a top-level `enforcement_mode` column. A first-class column would require a separate schema-extension bridge proposal with migration evidence.

## Out of Scope

- Promotion of `SHOULD` to `MUST` (deferred to a follow-on bridge slice gated on the parallel runtime thread per-parser empirical evidence).
- Promotion of `advisory` to `blocking` enforcement mode.
- Per-parser runtime implementation (parallel thread `gtkb-bridge-advisory-status-001`).
- Dashboard counter behavior (sibling thread `gtkb-advisory-report-dashboard-counters-spec`).
- ADVISORY status protocol definition (sibling thread `gtkb-advisory-report-protocol-extension`, VERIFIED at `-006`).
- ADVISORY report template (sibling thread `gtkb-advisory-report-template-spec`, GO at `-006`).

## Authority

- Codex GO: `bridge/gtkb-advisory-routing-dcl-004.md` (REVISED-1 review of `-003`).
- Parent Slice-0: `bridge/gtkb-advisory-report-message-type-conversion-004.md` (Codex GO at `-004`) - explicit follow-on (c) DCL-ADVISORY-ROUTING-001.
- Sibling VERIFIED: `bridge/gtkb-advisory-report-protocol-extension-006.md` (SHOULD wording aligned).
rows.title: ADVISORY entries route via Axis-2; cross-harness trigger excludes them by default

rows.assertions: [{"type": "grep", "file": "widget/src/components/Panel.tsx", "pattern": "ConsentBanner", "min_count": 1, "description": "I1: ConsentBanner is rendered by Panel.tsx (ordering enforced by tests/widget/test_widget_consent_ordering.py)."}, {"type": "file_exists", "file": "tests/unit/test_widget_otp_verification.py", "description": "I2: OTP verification regression suite exists (email + phone gates)."}, {"type": "file_exists", "file": ".github/workflows/accessibility.yml", "description": "I3: axe-core WCAG 2.1 AA CI workflow exists."}, {"type": "file_exists", "file": "tests/flows/test_flow_auth_boundaries.py", "description": "I4: tenant-isolation auth boundary tests exist."}, {"type": "grep", "file": "widget/package.json", "pattern": "@pact-foundation/pact", "min_count": 1, "description": "I5: Pact contract tooling remains a declared dependency."}, {"type": "grep", "file": "widget/tsconfig.json", "pattern": "\"strict\": true", "min_count": 1, "description": "I6: widget TypeScript config retains strict mode."}]
rows.change_reason: Claude Design GUI-Refresh Intake implementation bridge — D1–D7 KB artifacts per GO'd bridge -002 binding conditions.
rows.description:
Machine-checkable invariants that MUST survive any Claude-Design-driven
refresh of the Agent Red widget. This is a NEW topic-specific governance
artifact (per Codex review F2 preference), cross-linked to — not modifying —
GOV-01 (CLAUDE.md line budget), GOV-09 (owner input classification), and
GOV-12 (WI triggers tests).

Future per-feature Claude Design refresh bridges MUST cite this artifact and
demonstrate how the refresh satisfies each invariant, or propose an explicit
owner-ratified exception. The assertion runner (``tools/knowledge-db/
assertions.py``) checks these at session start and on the pre-build gate.

Invariants (I1–I6)
------------------
* **I1** ConsentBanner renders before any chat message post-init. Tenant-
  configurable via ``consent_collection_enabled``; the ordering is structural
  in ``widget/src/components/Panel.tsx`` (ConsentBanner block precedes
  MessageList). Evidence: ``tests/widget/test_widget_consent_ordering.py``.
* **I2** OTP-gated flows require both email and phone verification where
  specified. Evidence: ``tests/unit/test_widget_otp_verification.py``,
  ``tests/chat/test_identity_preprocessor.py``.
* **I3** axe-core CI gate passes at WCAG 2.1 AA. Evidence: the workflow
  ``.github/workflows/accessibility.yml`` exists and is referenced by D6.
* **I4** Tenant isolation preserved. Evidence:
  ``tests/flows/test_flow_auth_boundaries.py``.
* **I5** Pact contracts unchanged unless explicitly re-generated. Evidence:
  ``widget/package.json`` depends on ``@pact-foundation/pact``.
* **I6** Widget builds clean under strict TypeScript. Evidence:
  ``widget/tsconfig.json`` declares ``strict`` mode; ``widget/package.json``
  exposes ``tsc --noEmit`` via the ``typecheck`` script.

Scope boundary
--------------
This spec is the authority for Claude-Design-driven refresh invariants only.
It does NOT redefine ordering for other UI workstreams, and it does NOT
authorize any widget/source change on its own — changes still require a
per-feature bridge with explicit Codex GO.
rows.title: Claude Design Refresh Preservation Contract

rows.change_reason: S158 audit: adding description to protected behavior spec
rows.description: When a tenant has an active widget configuration (activation status is 'active' or a valid draft has been activated), the Agent Red chat widget must render on the standalone admin console dashboard. The widget component reads activation status from the tenant's configuration API and conditionally renders. This is a core product behavior — if the widget doesn't display when active, the customer cannot preview their chat experience. [Source: admin/shared/AnalyticsOverview.tsx]
rows.title: Widget displays on admin console when Active

rows.change_reason: Remove 1 dead-pattern assertion(s) (integrity scan Fix 2)
rows.description: The standalone admin console must display the Agent Red favicon (the {r} curly brace icon) in the browser tab. The favicon is loaded from /favicon.ico or the brand assets directory. This is a branding requirement that ensures professional appearance. [Source: admin/standalone/index.html]
rows.title: Favicon on standalone admin console

rows.change_reason: Remove 1 dead-pattern assertion(s) (integrity scan Fix 2)
rows.description: The provider (SPA) admin console must display the Agent Red favicon in the browser tab, matching the standalone admin's branding. [Source: admin/provider/index.html]
rows.title: Favicon on provider admin console

rows.assertions: [{"type": "grep", "pattern": "Save your configuration first", "file": "src/multi_tenant/activation_service.py", "min_count": 2, "description": "Friendly error message (2+ occurrences)"}]
rows.change_reason: S158 audit: adding description to protected behavior spec
rows.description: When a tenant attempts to activate a widget but no draft configuration exists, the system must return a user-friendly error message rather than a generic 500 or cryptic error. The activation endpoint checks for draft existence before proceeding and returns a descriptive 400-level error. [Source: src/multi_tenant/activation_service.py]
rows.title: User-friendly 'no draft to activate' error

rows.change_reason: S158 audit: adding description to protected behavior spec
rows.description: When saving Memory & Privacy settings, fields gated behind higher tiers (e.g., Layer 3 cross-session learning for Professional+, Layer 4 dedicated training for Enterprise) must be filtered out for lower-tier tenants before persisting to Cosmos DB. This prevents tenants from enabling features they haven't paid for via direct API manipulation. [Source: src/multi_tenant/mutation_executor.py]
rows.title: Memory & Privacy tier-gated fields filtered on save

rows.change_reason: S158 audit: adding description to protected behavior spec
rows.description: When a superadmin invites a team member, an invitation email must be sent to the invitee's email address. The email is sent via the ACS email service (with Titan SMTP fallback) and contains the admin dashboard URL. Failure to send the email should not block the invitation record creation. [Source: src/multi_tenant/endpoints.py]
rows.title: Team invite email sent to invitee
